title: Versality for the relative Fukaya category
speaker: Nick Sheridan
tags: Homological Mirror Symmetry: Methods and Structures

proof_stages.label: Batyrev/Greene-Plesser mirror-symmetry target
proof_stages.summary: The talk first formulates the toric hypersurface mirror family and states the intended homological mirror-symmetry theorem for Greene-Plesser/Batyrev mirror pairs over the Novikov field.

proof_stages.label: Relative Kähler moduli near a divisor
proof_stages.summary: For a compact complex manifold with simple normal crossings divisor, relative Kähler forms are organized by the ample cone in relative cohomology, with Seidel's picture identifying the boundary/deepest degeneration with the Fukaya category of the divisor complement.

proof_stages.label: Relative Fukaya category as a deformation family
proof_stages.summary: The monoid-completed coefficient ring R(N), its Novikov specializations, and the relative Fukaya category are defined so that fibers recover the absolute Fukaya category at Kähler points and the affine Fukaya category of X\D at the toric zero point.

proof_stages.label: First-order divisor deformation classes
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains deformations of the affine Fukaya category and identifies the first-order Hochschild classes coming from holomorphic disks intersecting exactly one divisor component.

proof_stages.label: Versality criterion via closed-open maps
proof_stages.summary: Versality is defined, and the main theorem asserts that under niceness, Calabi-Yau/torsion c1, H^2-vanishing, and closed-open surjectivity hypotheses, the chosen relative Fukaya subcategory is a versal deformation; the proof idea is that symplectic cohomology in degree two is generated by the divisor-linking orbits corresponding to the first-order classes.

proof_stages.label: Upgrade to homological mirror symmetry
proof_stages.summary: To prove HMS, one matches the central affine Fukaya subcategory with a perfect-complex subcategory on the mirror family and matches first-order classes, after which versality supplies the mirror map and split-generation upgrades the comparison to a quasi-equivalence of the full categories.

examples.name: Z/2 anti-holomorphic involution
examples.note: The speaker gives a common symmetry case in which a group action kills the equivariant part of H^2 of the complement, illustrating how the versality theorem's vanishing hypothesis can be relaxed rather than producing the theorem's main idea.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only cites the case of a Z/2 action by an anti-holomorphic involution as a useful way to make the equivariant part vanish, without analyzing or applying a specific involution.

passes.reason: An ordinary order-two anti-holomorphic symmetry is used as a helpful equivariance device to eliminate extra classes, not to refute a claim.
A Z/2 anti-holomorphic involution is used as a standard equivariant symmetry to eliminate certain cohomology classes, not as an obstruction or classification-exception.
A standard order-two anti-holomorphic group action is used as an equivariant workaround to make the relevant invariant cohomology vanish, not as a disproof or obstruction.
a standard order-two anti-holomorphic symmetry is used as a useful group action to kill equivariant classes, not to refute a claim
A Z/2 anti-holomorphic involution is a standard finite symmetry/action used to enforce equivariant vanishing, not a sporadic, limiting, extremal, or counterexample case.

A [0m[0m[0m[0mZ/2[0m anti-holomorphic involution is used as a useful equivariant symmetry to eliminate classes, not as a counterexample, and it is not structurally exceptional, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric.
Used as a useful ordinary symmetry action to make the equivariant part of H^2 vanish, not as an obstruction or sporadic/extreme object.

A standard order-two anti-holomorphic symmetry is used as a useful sufficient case for the equivariant-vanishing hypothesis, not to refute a claim, and it is not structurally exceptional, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric.

It is cited as a useful equivariant symmetry hypothesis making the relevant H^2 vanish, not as a refutation, and an order-two anti-holomorphic action is not structurally maximal, sporadic, degenerate, or extremal.

Used as a standard equivariant symmetry to make the relevant invariant H2 vanish, not as an obstruction or failure case; a Z/2 anti-holomorphic involution is not intrinsically maximal, sporadic, extremal, or degenerate.
It is cited as a useful ordinary Z/2 real-structure action to remove equivariant H^2 obstructions, not as a failure case or structurally maximal/exceptional/extreme/degenerate object.

A standard order-two anti-holomorphic symmetry is used as a useful group action to remove obstruction classes, not to refute a claim, and it is not sporadic, collapsed, extremal, or maximally symmetric.
